data.title: Nvidia: You're Running Out Of Time To Sell
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation’s (NVDA) stock trades at a price similar to which it was trading back in August and September, when we issued our latest Sell recommendations on the company. At the same time, since our latest coverage, the risks that Nvidia faces have only increased and now threaten to undermine its dominance over the AI business. Because of that, we have decided to maintain our Sell rating for the company, and in this article, we will highlight why Nvidia’s downside remains significant.

The Market Is Unimpressed

There’s no denying that Nvidia had a good performance in Q3, considering that its revenues during the quarter increased by 62.5% Y/Y to $57 billion and exceeded expectations. Despite such a performance, the company’s stock still depreciated following the release of the report last week and has also underperformed in the last month.

The decline of the share price was caused by the number of things. One of those things is the inability to access the growing Chinese market, which limits Nvidia’s overall upside. During the latest earnings call, Nvidia noted that the sizable purchases of its H20 chips that were developed specifically for the Chinese market have never materialized. The company also didn’t forecast any growth in data center revenues in China in Q4. This is a problem because the Chinese market alone could’ve represented a $50 billion opportunity for Nvidia in 2025. While there are reports that the Trump administration might allow Nvidia to sell even more powerful chips in China, there’s no guarantee that the company would be able to greatly benefit from that decision in Q4 and beyond.

After all, in August, the Chinese state outlets reported that H20 chips are not safe to use. Then, in September, the Chinese regulators banned the purchases of Nvidia’s chips by Chinese companies. One of the reasons behind Beijing’s decision to limit Nvidia’s presence in China is the fact that the country is on its way to becoming self-sufficient in the field of chip design and chip production. The strategists at UBS believe that in five years, the country’s self-sufficiency in the AI computing field could grow to 90%. At the same time, Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang himself initially said in an interview with Financial Times that China would win the AI race.

Considering that a couple of months ago, Huawei shared its 3-year plan to erode Nvidia’s dominance over the AI business, it’s possible that China indeed could become self-sufficient in the AI field over time. That is why it’s hard to see Nvidia expanding its data center business in China anytime soon. With the lack of access to the Chinese market and the rise of Chinese competition, Nvidia’s overall upside in the future could be limited.

At the same time, there’s also a possibility that Nvidia slowly starts to lose market share to competitors back at home. In recent years, hyperscalers have started to design their custom AI chips in order to decrease their reliance on Nvidia and cut costs. In October, Amazon announced that Anthropic, which is the creator of the popular Claude AI chatbot, is going to use 1 million of its custom chips by the end of this year. Then, earlier this week, Elon Musk said that Tesla has now entered the AI chip design business, while Meta is now considering using Google’s AI chips in its data centers in 2027. This suggests that the competition is heating up and there’s no guarantee that Nvidia will be able to continue to dominate the AI market in the upcoming years.

In addition to all of that, there’s also a risk that we’re going to witness the slowdown of capital expenditures on AI, which was responsible for the recent AI boom in the first place, in the foreseeable future. In recent months, the executives of companies like Google, Palantir, and OpenAI have been hinting at the possibility that some spending might be irrational and could yield limited results. They are not entirely wrong, considering that there’s an indication that the majority of companies are currently getting zero return on their artificial intelligence investments. This is why it’s not a surprise that the AI bubble fears are gaining traction and are changing the market sentiment.

The combination of all of the developments described above suggests that the further aggressive growth of Nvidia’s business is not guaranteed anymore. While the company had a good performance in Q3 and will likely end 2025 on a high note, there’s a risk that it could lose its dominance over the AI market in the year to come. This is why a further depreciation of Nvidia’s shares is possible in the foreseeable future.

The Real Value of Nvidia

As risks to Nvidia’s dominance over the AI business increase, we find it hard to justify its current price. The company’s major multiples are already significantly greater in comparison to the sector median multiples, which makes the stock a more risky investment in comparison to others.

In addition, our valuation model also shows that Nvidia is overvalued. When building the model, we used a 3% perpetual growth rate, which is close to the historical GDP and inflation rate. Our discount rate in the model is 12.53%. We figured it out by calculating Nvidia’s cost of debt and cost of equity. When calculating the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4%, a beta of 2.27, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

Thanks to the good performance in Q3 and the improvement of the guidance for Q4, we decided to greatly increase our sales growth rate for 2025 in comparison to the previous model, which is now similar to the consensus growth rate. After 2025, we expect a gradual normalization of the growth rate as the rising risks are likely to prevent Nvidia from growing at a triple-digit rate at which it was expanding its business in the not-so-distant past.

We have also slightly increased the EBIT margin in the model below. In the past, we assumed an EBIT margin of 58%. However, thanks to the good performance in Q3, the TTM EBIT margin is already at nearly 59%. This is why we also increased it in the model below to 59% for 2025. After that, we expect a gradual decrease in margins as the rising challenges that were described in this article earlier could affect the performance of the business. We also maintained the tax rate in the model at 15%, while the bottom part of the table didn’t change significantly in comparison to the previous model, and most of the assumptions there closely correlate with Nvidia’s past performance.

Our model shows that Nvidia’s enterprise value is $2.06 trillion, while its equity value is $2.11 trillion. That means that Nvidia’s intrinsic value is $86.05 per share, which is below the current market price by nearly 52%.

Our previous model from September showed that Nvidia’s intrinsic value is $65.34 per share. The reason why we’re seeing a bump in the intrinsic value in the new model above is that we have greatly increased Nvidia’s sales assumptions, which now closely correlate with the consensus. This was done in order to cover any potential upside that Nvidia might have in case some of our risks don’t materialize. However, even under the revised assumptions, Nvidia remains overvalued, and its stock has plenty of room to fall.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

We see two things that can undermine our bearish thesis. First of all, it’s possible that hyperscalers continue to spend record sums of money on scaling their AI infrastructure in 2026. If that happens, then it might take longer for the risks described in this article to materialize, while in the meantime, Nvidia’s stock might regain its momentum.

Secondly, the potential cut of interest rates in December might improve the market’s mood and change the sentiment for the better again. This could also help Nvidia’s shares to rebound.

However, those things could benefit Nvidia in the short to near-term, only in our opinion. In the long run, the company will likely lose its dominance over the AI market due to the combination of various developments described earlier in this article.

Final Thoughts

The fact that Nvidia’s stock depreciated following the release of the good earnings report for Q3 shows that the market has started to question the company’s ability to continue to dominate the AI business in light of the rising competition. In addition, the inability to access the Chinese market has already limited Nvidia’s overall upside in 2025, and that’s unlikely to change in 2026 and beyond, considering that Beijing aims to become more self-sufficient in the field of chip design and chip production. Also, the rising AI bubble fears have already derailed Nvidia’s stock momentum, while the potential AI CapEx bust could result in a significant depreciation of the company’s shares in the foreseeable future.

Considering all of those developments, we believe that maintaining the rating of Sell for Nvidia’s stock makes the most sense right now. This is especially true since the company is already overvalued at the current price.

data.title: Madison Diversified Income Fund Q3 2025 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
Market Recap - Third Quarter 2025

For all intents and purposes, the third quarter of 2025 was another exceptional period for investors, the S&P 500 was up another 8.1%, international stocks (MSCI ACWI ex-US Index) gained 6.9%, and bonds (Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index) tacked on 2.0%. However, despite the handsome absolute gains all-around, for those light on mega cap growth stocks, it felt like another “AI or die” quarter from a relative standpoint as the Magnificent-7 (Mag-7) and other AI associated stocks vastly outperformed in a continuation of the market action we’ve seen since the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022. The return differential was stark, the Mag-7 averaged a near 18.0% return versus the median S&P 500 stock up just 4.4%. Dividend-focused stocks were even further behind, with the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index returning just 4.0%.

Also notable was the performance of higher beta and lower quality stocks, evidenced by the 13.0% return on the S&P 500 High Beta Index (now up 64.0% off the April low!) and the 12.4% showing from the small cap Russell 2000 Index. Quality stocks were left behind in the quarter’s momentum thrust with the S&P 500 Quality Index up a solid, yet relatively pedestrian 3.1%.

Markets were further boosted by shifting expectations for easier monetary policy (interest rate cuts) from the Federal Reserve (Fed). July’s jobs report, released on August 1st, disappointed to the low side, but the kicker was in the large negative revisions to the May and June figures, where over 250,000 jobs were wiped away. The negative shock cemented a 0.25% rate cut in September and pushed up odds for an additional 0.50% of rate cuts over the remainder of the year. Interest rates fell and rate sensitive stocks, like small caps, jumped with 10.5% (85%) of the Russell 2000 Index’s 12.4% return coming in August/September. The fall in rates was even more beneficial to bonds, where 2.3% (113%) of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index’s 2.0% return was made in the final two months of the quarter.

Performance

The Madison Diversified Income Fund (MBLAX) trailed the benchmark for the quarter but remains well ahead of the bogey for 2025.

Equity performance was held back from underperformance by our covered call and MLP positions, somewhat offset by outperformance from our core active dividend equity holding.

Within fixed income, strong performance from our holdings in investment grade corporate bonds and mortgage-backed securities boosted performance. Finally, our active aggregate bond holding was an outperformer, while our active short-term position underperformed.

Outlook

As we move into 2025’s final act, we marvel at the S&P 500 Index’s astonishing 35% run off the April low. A ricochet trajectory not typically seen outside the aftermath of a violent bear market. It appears we’ve reached a point where investors’ level of exuberance can only be rivaled by their level of cognitive dissonance. We commented last quarter that markets have been trading like uncertainty has been eradicated, yet it most certainly remains. The economic distortions of the tariff saga are immense, and the ripple effects are only now just starting to come to light as the deliberations and trade deals carried on until early August. Investors are all-in on AI, yet there is little evidence that the hundreds of billions of dollars in capital thrown at the industry have a viable path to substantial revenue generation.

The Fed’s focus has pivoted away from price stability to a weakening labor market despite inflation reaccelerating, continuing to increase at a rate 50% above their target, and their collective wisdom not seeing a return to 2% until 2028. The U.S. equity market trading at nearly 23x 12 months forward earnings doesn’t appear to believe much, if anything can go wrong. The same can be seen in the corporate bond market where interest rate spreads over U.S. Treasuries (credit spreads) sit near historic lows.

Gold continues to send a cautionary message, up 16% in the quarter and has gained over 40% in 2025 alone. Investors typically turn to the precious metal for crisis insurance and/or a hedge against inflation/currency depreciation. Clearly the shifting geopolitical landscape and deteriorating U.S. government financial position, with no signs of fiscal restraint, are spooking a large contingent of market participants. We heed the message.

Resilient consumer spending is holding recession risk in check. There is no doubt that the labor market has slowed, but it’s not showing undue signs of stress. Turnover has stalled, with very little hiring or firing happening in the aggregate. To us, this is another sign that uncertainty remains in the minds of business leaders awaiting better clarity on the economy. However, until we see a material rise in unemployment claims, the U.S. economy will likely remain stable.

In conclusion, it should be clear that we don’t share the market’s view of a goldilocks future, yet we’re cautiously optimistic. We see pockets of the U.S. equity market as teetering on irrational exuberance, but there are opportunities in companies beyond the AI sphere where valuations are less demanding. Given the uncertain backdrop, elevated valuations, and investor overconfidence, we continue to stress higher quality assets and remain calculating in taking risk. True to our participate and protect philosophy, we don’t think it’s a good time to be to be swinging for the fence.

Respectfully,

Patrick Ryan

Stuart Dybdahl

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000® Index, which represents approximately 11% of the total market capitalization of the Russell 3000® Index.

The S&P 500® High Beta Index measures the performance of 100 constituents in the S&P 500 that are most sensitive to changes in market returns.

The S&P 500® Quality Index is designed to track high quality stocks in the S&P 500 by quality score, which is calculated based on return on equity, accruals ratio and financial leverage ratio.

data.title: TCW MetWest Sustainable Securitized Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Navigating a Shifting Curve

The third quarter of 2025 unfolded against a backdrop of shifting monetary policy and resilient risk appetite. After a summer marked by weakening labor market signals- underwhelming payroll prints and downward revisions, the Federal Reserve delivered a 25 bps “risk-management” rate cut in September to stave off further deterioration. This decision followed months of debate within the FOMC, including a rare two-Governor dissent in July, and came alongside projections for two additional cuts by year-end. Rate moves reflected this pivot: 1-year and 2-year yields fell by 35 bps and 11 bps, while longer maturities underperformed, with the 10-year and 30-year down just 8 bps and 4 bps amid lingering uncertainty over tariffs and Fed independence. Despite these headwinds, investors focused on the valuation benefits of lower rates rather than their underlying cause, fueling strong returns across risk assets. Equities surged, with the S&P 500 up 8% and the NASDAQ gaining over 11%,while fixed income markets posted solid gains-the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index rose 2%, delivering nearly 50 bps of excess return as spreads tightened broadly.

Within securitized credit, performance was positive but highly differentiated. Agency MBS led the sector with a 2.4% gain, supported by declining rate volatility late in the quarter, even as spreads widened modestly earlier due to elevated origination activity and payment uncertainty. Non-agency RMBS advanced across collateral types, buoyed by seasonal borrower credit strength, low refinancing incentives, and slower prepayment speeds that preserved carry and yield profiles. CMBS returned 1.8%, with private-label deals outperforming amid a risk-on tone, though office-heavy tranches continued to face structural pressure, contrasting with resilience in multifamily, industrial, and data center properties. ABS markets were generally stable, posting 1.6% returns, though traditional auto and credit card collateral lagged while CLOs benefitted from strong demand for yield premiums, outperforming corporates of similar quality and maturity. The unifying theme was value dispersion driven by rate volatility and property-type differences, creating opportunities for active managers to add value through disciplined credit selection and tactical positioning as the path of rates becomes clearer heading into year-end.

Performance

The TCW MetWest Sustainable Securitized Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 1.94% net of fees in the third quarter of 2025, finishing 49 bps behind the Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities Index (“Index") and bringing year-to-date outperformance to 3 bps. The primary headwind to relative performance stemmed from the Fund's structural underweight to agency MBS, which rallied as the Fed resumed its rate-cutting cycle, easing interest rate volatility. However, the Fund's emphasis on belly and lower coupons partially mitigated this drag, as these coupons outperformed higher coupons amid increased prepayment activity that exacerbated negative convexity concerns in higher coupons.

Diversified exposure to off- Index securitized products contributed positively to performance. Allocations to non-agency MBS were beneficial as spreads tightened and prices rose across both legacy and newer vintage issues, supported by stable borrower credit and slower prepayment speeds. The Fund's sizeable position in non-agency CMBS added further value, particularly through its focus on single asset single borrower (SASB) structures, which continued to perform well against the broader risk-on backdrop and strong sponsorship of performing properties such as multifamily and industrial assets. Finally, the ABS allocation proved advantageous, with the largest gains coming from CLOs and non-traditional collateral types including data centers, fiber networks, and clean energy projects- sectors that offered attractive yield premiums and benefited from robust investor demand.

As of quarter-end, nearly 88% of the portfolio carries TCW ESG Flags, reflecting meaningful alignment with environmental and social objectives. Social-focused investments represent the largest share at 51.5%, driven by Agency MBS and RMBS pools supporting affordable housing and responsible lending practices. Green investments account for 27.2%, primarily through CMBS exposure to properties with energy efficiency certifications and SASB structures meeting green building standards. ABS allocations include clean energy projects and ESG-linked CLOs, reinforcing the Fund's commitment to combining strong risk-adjusted returns with positive sustainability outcomes

Outlook & Positioning

We entered the year recognizing a wider range of potential economic outcomes, with a bias toward slower growth. While the outlook still points to moderation, the sequencing of monetary policy has become critical. The Fed's recent pivot toward easing- following signs of labor market weakness- has helped stabilize rate volatility and improve liquidity across securitized markets. For the Fund, this environment reinforces the importance of positioning in sectors that offer structural resilience and attractive relative value. Agency MBS remains a core allocation given its liquidity and government guarantee, though spreads have been sensitive to origination trends and payment uncertainty. Non-agency MBS and CMBS continue to present compelling opportunities, supported by strong collateral fundamentals and sponsorship in segments such as multifamily and industrial properties, even as office exposure remains under structural pressure. ABS markets remain broadly stable, with non-traditional collateral types like data centers and clean energy offering incremental yield and diversification. Despite these tailwinds, risk assets broadly reflect optimistic assumptions, with credit spreads near historic lows and limited buffers against volatility. This underscores the need for disciplined underwriting and selective exposure to sectors where fundamentals remain robust. Our focus remains on securities that combine strong relative value with durable credit profiles, while maintaining flexibility to navigate an uncertain macro backdrop. Expectations for further rate cuts and a gradual slowdown inform our slightly longer-than-index duration stance and overweight to the front end of the curve, positioning the Fund to benefit from declining rates while preserving risk-adjusted return potential.

As value investors, TCW remains vigilant in assessing risks and opportunities across markets. While corporate spread levels present an asymmetric risk/reward profile, securitized products continue to offer a more compelling relative value argument, making them an attractive segment for allocating portfolio risk budgets. Agency MBS represents the largest position, concentrated in lower and belly coupons for pull-to-par potential and better call protection. The Fund maintains meaningful allocations to non-agency MBS, including legacy re-performing loans and manufactured housing, which align with social objectives. CMBS exposure is focused on SASB deals, including high-quality properties with energy efficiency certifications and U.S. green bonds compliant with European Union Securities Regulations. ABS positions reflect targeted allocations to mezzanine and AAA CLO tranches, as well as high-quality non-traditional collateral types like data centers, fiber, and sustainable energy. Across all asset types, the team continues to seek securities that combine strong relative value with environmental and social characteristics. Finally, expectations for rates to continue moving lower- and for the Fed to move more quickly should labor conditions weaken further - inform a slightly longer-than-index duration (+0.2 years) and an overweight to the front end of the curve, positioning the Fund to benefit from declining rates while preserving flexibility to navigate volatility.

GLOSSARY OF TERMS

AAA Rating- Rating issued to investment-grade debt that has a high level of creditworthiness with the strongest capacity to repay investors. Agency MBS- The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Asset-Backed Securities-A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Basis Point (BPS)- One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index or “the Agg”- Is a broad-based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) as a benchmark to measure their relative performance. Bond-A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)- A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations)- A special purpose vehicle (SPV) with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. Collateral- Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Convexity- A measure of the curvature in the relationship between bond prices and bond yields that demonstrates how the duration of a bond changes as the interest rate changes. If a bond's duration increases as yields increase, the bond is said to have negative convexity. If a bond's duration rises and yields fall, the bond is said to have positive convexity. Corporate- Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Coupon- The interest rate stated on a bond when it's issued. The coupon is typically paid semiannually. Duration- A measure of the sensitivity of the price(the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices Excess Return- The rate of return on a security or an investment portfolio in excess of the return generated by a portfolio of U.S. Treasury securities with similar duration and partial durations. Expense Ratio- A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Federal Reserve (the Fed)- The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. Floating Rate- Any interest rate that changes on a periodic basis. The change is usually tied to movement of an outside indicator, such as the prime interest rate. High Yield- A bond that is rated below investment grade. Liquidity- The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities)- A type of asset-backed security that is secured by a mortgage or collection of mortgages. These securities must also be grouped in one of the top two ratings as determined by a accredited credit rating agency, and usually pay periodic payments that are similar to coupon payments. Furthermore, the mortgage must have originated from a regulated and authorized financial institution. Mezzanine Investment- Mezzanine investment financing (also referred to as third-stage capital) refers to a later stage investment provided to a company that is already producing and selling a product or service, for the purpose of helping the company achieve a critical objective that, in many cases, will enable it to go public. Mezzanine investment financing provides for major expansion in companies whose sales are increasing, and whose cash flow is break-even or slightly positive. Mutual Funds- An investment vehicle that is made up of a pool of funds collected from many investors for the purpose of investing in securities such as stocks, bonds, money-market instruments and similar assets. Mutual funds are operated by money managers, who invest the fund's capital and attempt to produce capital gains and income for the fund's investors. A mutual fund's portfolio is structured and maintained to match the investment objectives stated in its prospectus. Non-agency Mortgage backed securities made up of mortgage loans that are not guaranteed by a government-supported agency. Non-Agency MBS- Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Overweight- A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. S&P 500- The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor's 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. SASB- The Sustainability Accounting Standards Board is a non-profit organization founded in 2011, creates and maintains industry-specific standards that guide companies' disclosure of financially material sustainability information to investors and other financial stakeholders. Securitized- When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Spread- The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Tariff- A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Tranches- Are segments created from a pool of securities, usually debt instruments such as bonds or mortgages, that are divided up by risk, time to maturity, or other characteristics in order to be marketable to different investors. Each portion or tranche of a securitized or structured product is one of several related securities offered at the same time, but with varying risks, rewards and maturities to appeal to a diverse range of investors. U.S. Green Bond- A fixed income debt instrument in which an issuer (typically a corporation, government, or financial institution) borrows a large sum of money from investors for use in sustainability-focused projects. U.S. Treasuries (U.S. Treasury Securities)- Bills, notes and bonds that are debt obligations of the U.S. government. Valuations- The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility- A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield- The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment's cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Premium- Additional return.

data.title: Milestones Over Sales: The Sanofi Partnership Now Drives Novavax's Outlook
data.description:
Thesis

Novavax, Inc. (NVAX) managed to report a 3Q25 GAAP EPS loss of –$1.25, missing estimates by $0.33. Revenue came in at $70.45 million, which actually beat expectations by $25.58 million despite falling 16.6% year over year. The company also reiterated its FY25 revenue framework of $1.04 billion to $1.06 billion, roughly in line with the $1.05 billion consensus. As you would expect, the market didn’t take these earnings too well, and shares have been on the decline since the announcement, down about 14% since November 6th. In general, shares have seen a big pullback since October, when we once saw the stock trading in the $9.50 range. You can now enter the stock for $6.50.

The market is also beginning to see just how dependent Novavax is on Sanofi (SNY) for revenue. Sanofi now leads global commercialization of Nuvaxovid and is set to drive future royalties and milestone payments. Even the company’s most meaningful upcoming growth catalysts, especially the upcoming COVID-flu combination vaccine, are fully dependent on Sanofi’s execution and market reach.

Lackluster Earnings

Revenue for the quarter came in at roughly $70 million, actually down from $85 million we saw a year earlier. This was largely due to the transition of commercial responsibility for Nuvaxovid to Sanofi and some lower-than-expected direct product sales. Direct product sales fell quite sharply to $13 million. However, the Sanofi partnership remained a big driving force on earnings. Novavax recognized about $48 million from Sanofi in 3Q25 and has now achieved $225 million in Sanofi-related milestones year-to-date. This would also include $50 million tied to marketing authorization transfers.

The company also benefited nicely from strengthening partner activity globally, which includes Takeda Pharmaceutical Company Limited's (TAK) approval of Nuvaxovid in Japan and good progress of the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine. That vaccine has now sold 25 million doses since launch. Operating expenses also continued to shift more in line with the company’s new R&D-centric model. SG&A expenses fell 55% year-over-year, as we expected after commercial infrastructure reductions.

On the other hand, R&D spending rose to help out with their early-stage pipeline advancement, though nearly half of these R&D costs were essentially reimbursed by Sanofi. A big talking point on earnings, which seemed to overshadow the quarter, was $126 million in non-cash charges tied to Maryland facility consolidation and debt refinancing. This figure contributed to a wider net loss of $202 million. Still, Novavax managed to exit the quarter with a hefty $778 million in cash and reaffirmed its previous financial guidance while raising its full-year revenue framework. Management pointed to improving visibility from licensing, hitting some clinical milestones, and also expanding Matrix-M partnerships. So even though the company is navigating declining standalone COVID-19 vaccine sales, we have a lot to look forward to going into 2026.

A Delay to Profitability

So, Novavax has updated its outlook for this quarter. The company also pushed its profitability target back by a full year to 2028. It’s a delay driven mainly by the weak demand for standalone COVID-19 vaccines I mentioned, but also the company’s dependence on future revenue tied to its partnership with Sanofi. As you would expect, COVID-19 vaccine prescriptions in the U.S. have declined; they’re down about 20% this season. It means Novavax has only managed to capture a 1% market share. This has been a major point of frustration for its second-largest shareholder, Shah Capital. They’ve come out and urged the board to consider a sale. However, with Sanofi now leading commercial efforts in the U.S. and EU, I think we can expect a meaningful boost coming in 2026, particularly from the upcoming launch of Sanofi’s COVID-19/influenza combo vaccine containing Nuvaxovid.

Now, royalties from this product and other broader Sanofi-led commercialization should jump quite sharply. The problem is that Novavax’s own 2026 adjusted revenue, excluding any Sanofi sales and royalties, is only projected at $185 to $205 million. It goes to show just how reliant the company has become on its partners. And yes, in 2025, Novavax raised its adjusted revenue outlook to $1.04 to $1.06 billion, but that was driven primarily by milestone payments rather than actual vaccine sales. Keep in mind, its 3Q25 net loss grew to $202 million because of the mounting non-cash charges tied to facility consolidation and debt refinancing. So if we take all this together, it seems Novavax is trying to absorb some near-term financial strain but is also betting on combination vaccines and licensing revenue to pick up. The timeline for that profitability is now slower than previously promised.

Why Dependence May Not Be Such a Good Thing

As you now know, ​​Novavax has a heavy reliance on Sanofi, and we’re seeing in the market sentiment just how much significant strategic and financial risk gets priced in because the company’s future revenue growth, profitability timeline, and market relevance now hinge almost entirely on decisions it no longer controls. Sanofi are now leading the commercialisation of Nuvaxovid, and they are also set to own the launch of the COVID-flu combination vaccine. Novavax is also dependent on things like Sanofi’s marketing priorities and even resource allocation. They are factors that could shift depending on Sanofi’s broader vaccine portfolio and their own internal goals, not Novavax’s.

So going forward, if Sanofi slows its investment or sees any regulatory delays elsewhere in its pipeline, it may result in it prioritising its own products. The broader COVID-19 vaccine market is struggling, and they're hoping a COVID-19/influenza combo shot will boost sales, but Novavax now has minimal ability to influence those sales execution or demand generation. Novavax also has quite limited alternative revenue streams, meaning any underperformance by Sanofi would see that net loss figure have more downside. They’ve already delayed their path to profitability once; if they don’t want to weaken investor confidence any more, they have to hit all targets leading into 2028. It's a big challenge for the company.

The Upside

The COVID-flu combination vaccine is where I see Novavax’s biggest upside driver. It's tipped to protect against both influenza and COVID-19 in just a single annual shot. Now, having two vaccines in a single shot is something that should be quite welcome by the public. It's no secret people don't like taking shots, so this would be something public-health agencies/pharmaceutical companies believe will significantly boost vaccination rates. The shot is set to use Novavax’s protein-based COVID-19 vaccine and Sanofi’s already well-established flu platform. It will then be paired with Novavax’s Matrix-M adjuvant to enhance immune response. Sanofi is currently leading all late-stage development and commercial planning. They're targeting a launch date sometime in 2026. Now, Sanofi’s global flu franchise is one of the largest in the world, so if approved, I see the adoption rates being high. It gives Novavax a potential high-margin royalty stream that could bring a bit of stability and growth to its revenue model.

Valuation

Novavax’s valuation now looks unusually cheap relative to its revenue base. Shares can also be picked up in the mid-$6 range, a big drop from the $9.5 we saw in October. They hold a forward EV to sales ratio of just 0.51x, far below the sector median of 3.78x. It’s a discount that shows a bit of a depressed sentiment and the company’s reliance on that milestone-driven revenue. Its forward price to earnings also paints a similar picture at 3.18x, dramatically lower than the sector’s 19.29x. There is obviously a lot of execution risk being priced in here. The market understands that the revenue now depends on the future Sanofi-led royalties rather than the actual earnings implied by 2025 guidance. The company has a market cap of nearly $1.05 billion but a cash figure of $763 million. It puts Novavax’s enterprise value at only about $540 million, which again goes to show just how heavily discounted the core business is. Over the past month, the chart has not looked very good for Novavax, with shares down about 23%, underperforming the S&P by quite a bit.

Looking Forward

Now, with such heavy dependence and a market of declining COVID-19 vaccine sales, I think it's best for investors to wait a bit before jumping back into Novavax stock. We need to see just how successful the combination vaccine therapy is before the stock starts to generate a bit of momentum. Keep in mind the company is not down on revenue; that figure actually beat on 3Q25, it is just that a large sum of that revenue is now dependent on Sanofi. Which is why I'm initiating my coverage with a hold and not a sell.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Underpriced 11% BDC Yield
data.description:
Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) remains my favorite BDC investment pick for 2026, following the company's Q3 earnings score card, despite marginally missing consensus NII estimates. The investment firm saw a sequential improvement in dividend coverage and significantly out-performed Ares Capital (ARCC) -- another previous top pick of mine -- in terms of portfolio growth, balance sheet quality, coverage ratio and NII trend in the last trailing-twelve months period.

Blackstone Secure Learning has the lowest non-accrual percentage in the BDC industry, at 0.1%, based off of fair value, and fully supported its dividend with net investment income in the September quarter. Given recent selling pressure in the market, including the BDC sector, shares now just trade at NAV (as opposed to a premium)... which creates an attractive investment setup for investors with a long term investment horizon.

Previous rating

In my last work on Blackstone Secured Lending I called the private credit platform a 'peace-of-mind-BDC' and rated it strong buy because it had a very small non-accrual ratio which is indicative of strong loan performance and low credit risks.

Blackstone Secured Lending benefited from a rebound in the dividend coverage ratio in the third-quarter, due to new loan originations, and maintained stable balance sheet quarter Q/Q. Since Blackstone Secured Lending also does better than Ares Capital on a number of metrics, I see BXSL as the superior investment option for investors seeking high-quality income.

A better high-yield BDC investment than Ares Capital

Blackstone Secured Lending is amongst the top three leading BDCs in terms of net asset size and the BDC has a portfolio that is heavily tilted toward first liens. These loans are highly secured, have low default risk and make recurring interest payments that are backing up the BDC's dividend obligations.

As of the end of the September quarter, Blackstone Secured Lending’s portfolio consisted to 97.5% of first liens (Ares Capital: 61.0%). Compared to other BDCs, Blackstone Secured Lending makes almost no equity investments, either in common or preferred stocks, and there has a much more stable, safer portfolio structure.

A key strength of Blackstone Secured Lending is not only that the company has a high degree of balance sheet quality, but also that it has been able to grow its portfolio consistently over time. In the third-quarter, Blackstone Secured Lending expanded its first lien originations significantly, based on its relationship network, leading to a 5.3% total portfolio growth rate Y/Y. Blackstone Secured Lending originated $571M in new loans which was the second consecutive quarter of positive growth for the BDC.

Better dividend coverage ratio mitigates interest rate risk

Blackstone Secured Lending has a material amount of variable rate-paying loans in its portfolio (98.6%) which is a risk for the BDC as the Federal Reserve gets ready to lower federal fund rates further next year. The consensus market expectation is for interest rates to drop to a range of 2.75% to 3.25% by the end of 2026, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

The current range of interest rates is 3.75% to 4.00% which means the Federal Reserve could be set to lower the federal fund rate 4 times next year, at 25 basis point increments.

While Blackstone Secured Lending has significant variable rate loan exposure and interest rate risk in a falling-rate world, the BDC is still growing its income on a total dollar basis: in Q3'25, the BDC's total investment income grew 4.7% year-over-year to $359M.

For comparison, Ares Capital's net investment income grew less then 1% year-over-year in Q3'25 to $782M. Blackstone Secured Lending's growth was due entirely to the firm’s 1) Stable non-accrual percentage, and 2) Net new loan originations that started to make positive NII contributions.

Blackstone Secured Lending also has a better NII trend than Ares Capital as well... which makes the BDC a preferred choice for investors. In the last twelve months, Blackstone Secured Lending’s net investment income per-share declined about 10% (due to an increase in shares outstanding) compared to a 16% drop-off rate for Ares Capital.

Stronger NII performance translates to better dividend coverage as welL: Blackstone Secured Lending had a dividend coverage ratio of 106.5% in Q3'25 vs. 100.0% for Ares Capital. In fact, both BDCs have seeing falling net investment income trends in the last year -- mostly due to falling interest incomes -- but the coverage trend is clearly better for Blackstone Secured Lending than it is for Ares Capital. Further, BXSL saw a sequential coverage improvement of 6.5 PP vs. a 2.1 PP decline for Ares Capital.

I do not expect a dividend cut for Blackstone Secured Lending at this point, given its excess dividend coverage, but see growing risks for Ares Capital. The latter may avoid a dividend cut if the BDC restores some of its non-accrual to performing status and originates more higher-quality, income-producing first liens going forward.

Underpriced 11% yield

High-quality BDCs like BXSL and ARCC have historically traded at higher P/NAV ratios than now, and Blackstone Secured Lending certainly fits this mold: the investment firm's shares are trading at net asset value exactly and 7% below their historical valuation average of 1.07X P/NAV.

Blackstone Secured Lending's shares are also slightly cheaper than those of Ares Capital despite out-performing the BDC on a number of metrics that matter to dividend investors the most, like non-accruals (balance sheet quality), net investment income and dividend coverage.

While falling interest rates may impact the BDC's net investment income in 2026, if rates drop as expected, the BDC has a history of paying dividends reliably and recent portfolio trends don't show a weakening of portfolio quality either.

As a fair value estimate for Blackstone Secured Lending I calculate ~$29.16/share which is the long-term, 5-year P/NAV ratio of 1.07X applied to its last net asset value of $27.15/share (as of September 30, 2025). The BDC's shares currently pay $3.08/share annually which calculates to a yield of ~11.3%. BXSL currently trades about 7% below my fair value estimate making it a good deal for investors, in my opinion. If shares revalue to my medium to long-term price target of $29.16, BXSL could have a fair yield of ~10%.

Risks with BXSL and other variable rate BDCs

The Federal Reserve cut federal fund rates in September by 25 basis points and more rate cuts are likely. This is set to hurt BDC's with large variable rate-paying loans in their portfolios (which tend to be most of them). What would change my mind about BXSL is if the investment firm were to see a deterioration of its dividend coverage ratio below 100% for more than two consecutive quarters or if the BDC suffered a rise in its non-accrual ratio above 1.0%.

Final thoughts

While the continuation of federal fund rate cuts in 2026 poses a risk to Blackstone Secured Lending's net investment income trajectory, I believe the exceptionally strong balance sheet quality and excess coverage will continue to protect the dividend. I also like Blackstone Secured Lending better than Ares Capital now given the BDC's strong track record of growing its portfolio and total investment income, while the lower-than-average valuation is yet another reason to remain bullish on the investment firm. Additionally, Blackstone Secured Lending's portfolio structure looks superior to me given a very high investment percentage in first liens. I currently do not expect a dividend cut and BXSL remains a top BDC investment pick for me with an underpriced 11% dividend yield.

data.title: Super Micro: Oversold, Just Above Strong Support Level
data.description:
Super Micro Computer's (SMCI) share price was $47 when I last covered this stock with a bullish article. The stock reached $60 in early October for the third time in 2025, but once again saw a sharp share price pivot since then. SMCI is currently closer to $30, which is a strong support level, as we can see from the share price chart.

I reiterate Strong Buy for SMCI not only because it currently trades close to a strong support level but also due to fundamental factors. With its current $19 billion market cap and the recent FY2026 revenue guidance boost to $36 billion, the P/S ratio is just slightly above 0.5 while the sector median is 3. The upgraded revenue guidance suggests that SMCI is still strong in capitalizing on AI tailwinds.

Reasons to continue investing in SMCI

Besides the aforementioned strong support level of $30, there is another meaningful technical indicator that suggests bullish setup. SMCI's 14-day RSI indicator currently stands at around 31, meaning that the stock is massively oversold. Last time SMCI's stock was oversold (early September) it was the beginning of a solid bull run from $40 to above $55. Overall, the below chart suggests that SMCI tends to demonstrate sharp share price rebounds after rapid RSI indicator drops.

There are solid positives from the fundamental perspective as well. I think that the biggest favorable development is the upgraded FY2026 revenue outlook during the current earnings season. The management now expects the fiscal year's sales to be $36 billion, up from $33 billion outlined in the previous revenue guidance. Furthermore, SMCI's expected full-year revenue of $36 billion will be around 64% higher compared to its FY2025 sales of roughly $22 billion. Such dynamics mean that SMCI is expanding its market share because its revenue is growing significantly faster than the industry. The management's revenue guidance upgrade for FY2026 made SMCI's forward P/S ratio exceptionally low.

SMCI's forward P/S ratio is just above 0.5, and is almost the same as it was in late 2024 when the stock crashed after the stock faced a cascade of negative events including resignation of EY as SMCI's auditor, potential Nasdaq delisting, and a DoJ inquiry into the company. With all these risks in late 2024, such a low P/S ratio was justified. However, we see no such risks and problems at the moment, but SMCI's valuation is currently like it is facing significant regulatory and accounting-related risks again. The forward P/E ratio setup also speaks bullishly, as SMCI's estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio is below 10.

The upgraded FY2026 revenue guidance not only signals a potential P/S ratio contraction but also underscores SMCI's strong ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds. During the Q3 earnings season, some of the largest players with direct AI exposure announced they would continue increasing their investments in data centers. The market's reaction was quite nervous at the time, as the payback timing from these vast data center investments remained uncertain. Consequently, there weren't many headlines about new large AI infrastructure projects over the last few weeks. I even heard the opinion that, due to the market's pessimism regarding increased AI capex plans, big tech leaders might have reconsidered their aggressive strategies. However, recent news dismissed all this pessimism. Amazon announced it will invest $15 billion in a new data center campus project in Northern Indiana and up to $50 billion in AI and supercomputing infrastructure for US agencies. Also, Microsoft signed a 95.7MW power purchase agreement in Spain to power its large prospective data centers in that country. Finally, it was announced that Google signed a sovereign cloud agreement with NATO. While the contract amount is not disclosed, given we are speaking about an organization of NATO's caliber, I believe it could be a multi-billion-dollar contract.

Yes, the payback duration from all these data centers is still uncertain. However, we have to keep in mind that SMCI is a "picks and shovels" play among this data center spending spree because server racks are vital for data centers. SMCI does everything needed to maintain its strategic positioning. Nvidia dominates the data center chips industry, and SMCI makes sure that its server racks are perfectly compatible with Nvidia and they are really fast in releasing new updates to their products that will deliver performance together with Nvidia's latest releases. And even if somehow AMD disrupts Nvidia's dominance in AI accelerators, SMCI will still be there to capitalize, as they ensure their offerings work efficiently with AMD's products as well.

Reasons to be bearish

SMCI's relatively weak current profitability dynamic is explained by the extremely fierce competition in the AI server industry. For example, there are other large reputable vendors like Dell (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE). Fierce competition forces all players to ramp up R&D spending while not being able to exercise significant pricing power. While the management said during the earnings call that they are striving to drive profitability improvements, they did not share a clear roadmap of how higher profitability will be achieved. My bet on SMCI's ability to drive profitability improvements over the long-term is based on the strong track-record of margins expansion before the AI revolution that started in early 2023. However, past performance does not guarantee future results.

The fact that SMCI's latest earnings release was marked with a dual miss is another reason to be cautious. It could have been not a big problem if it was just a lone hiccup, but SMCI's earnings surprise record continues to be weak with multiple EPS and revenue misses over the last couple of years. It is a bearish factor because without a strong earnings surprise record, it is difficult to gain investors' confidence. Without investors' confidence, the demand for SMCI can remain weak, which is adverse for the share price.

Furthermore, we see that the broader market's optimism regarding AI has cooled down over the last few months. There are questions about the sustainability of valuations of companies like Nvidia. As a result, despite a strong Q3 earnings release last week, Nvidia's share price continued demonstrating relative weakness. If performance of the largest AI names like NVDA continues to be weak, it is difficult to imagine that SMCI's share price will move in the opposite direction and demonstrate growth. Therefore, the market's sentiment around AI might be a solid headwind for SMCI.

The verdict

I think that there are far more reasons to remain bullish rather than switching to neutral or bearish. As a result, I reiterate Strong Buy for SMCI.

data.title: Gilt Yields Fall After Autumn Budget Despite Lack Of 2026 Tax Hikes
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK, Chris Turner, Global Head of Markets and Regional Head of Research for UK & CEE and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Chaotic release

The UK’s Autumn Budget is out after a chaotic early release – and for investors, the news is not entirely welcome.

Gilt yields initially fell as initial leaked headlines of the Office for Budget Responsibility’s assessment of the Treasury’s plan began to emerge. The level of headroom – the margin for error surrounding the government’s main fiscal rule to balance day-to-day spending with tax revenues by 2029 – has increased to £22bn, from £9bn in the spring.

But beneath the surface, the story is more nuanced.

For all the talk of a significant fiscal hole over recent weeks, the OBR’s economic forecast revisions only shaved £6bn off the government’s headroom. A well-flagged 0.3pp per year cut in the UK’s productivity growth was heavily offset by near-term upgrades to inflation/wage growth. We’d expected the shortfall from economic downgrades to total at least £15bn.

So a smaller fiscal hole, which means the degree of fiscal consolidation required was more modest, too. While tax hikes add up to an additional £26bn/year in revenue by 2029/30 – 0.75% of GDP - virtually none of this will come through in 2026.

That is absolutely key. Investors had already begun to reach that conclusion after the U-turn on hiking income tax a couple of weeks ago; gilt yields rose by 21bp in the immediate few days that followed, before falling back thereafter. A more significant fall in yields today would have required a greater share of the tax hikes to kick in immediately.

Tax threshold freezes – a key plank of this budget – are already frozen until 2028. Extending them into the next decade will raise an additional £8bn, but not immediately. Changes to pension taxation and a levy on high value properties are similarly delayed. For investors, it does beg the question of whether these policies get watered down - particularly with the next election set to coincide with many of these planned tax hikes.

Zooming out a bit though, the UK’s public finances are set to improve next year – and the Autumn Budget doesn’t change that. Public Sector Net Borrowing is forecast to fall from 4.5% this year to 3.5% next, in large part because of the long-standing freeze in income tax thresholds. Importantly, the government has resisted pressure to increase departmental budgets in 2026 more aggressively, too. Those budgets will increase by 1.8% in real terms next year, considerably less generous than the 3.3/3.4% increases seen through FY2024 and 2025.

Add that all together, and the Debt Management Office has confirmed that it expects issuance to be lower in FY2026.

BoE December rate cut remains likely

For the Bank of England, the Autumn Budget doesn’t materially alter the case for further rate cuts. A more material fiscal tightening in 2026 would have argued more convincingly for offsetting monetary easing.

We do still think a December rate cut remains likely, though. Cuts to energy bills will lower headline inflation in 2026, even if not significantly – perhaps by 0.3pp. That’s helpful at a time when the Bank’s hawks are worried that high rates of inflation today could fuel inflation expectations, keeping price pressures elevated well into the future. But recent inflation and wage data has hinted that those concerns are overblown. We expect three more cuts from the BoE, including one in December, and that view hasn’t changed as a result of the Budget.

That leaves the million-pound question: will the Chancellor have to come back for more tax hikes in 2026? Ever since last year’s Budget, we felt tax hikes this year were inevitable. Next year, we’re less sure.

The Chancellor has left herself with a greater buffer after the fiscal rules – rules which will become more helpful for her by the time of the next budget. It’s an underappreciated point, but from 2026, the Treasury’s rules will allow Reeves to run a 0.5% current budget deficit by the end of the forecast horizon. That will add another £17bn to the fiscal headroom. Unless the economic outlook turns sour, that raises the bar for further tax hikes next year.

Yet the UK’s public finances remain precarious. The DMO projects Gilt issuance to remain close to, or even above, £300bn for each of the next three years. And given the strain on public services, political pressure to top up day-to-day spending will inevitably grow. Current plans have departmental budgets rising by an average of 0.6% per year in 2027-30.

Adding extra cash may well require a fresh conversation about tax. And with many of the smaller tax levers already pulled in this and the last Autumn Budget, it will get harder to raise more revenue without touching one of the major taxes.

Gilt investors were satisfied today, but fiscal concerns not over

The knee-jerk reaction in Gilt yields just after the leaking of the OBR report and also during earlier Budget rumours underline the markets’ sensitivity to the UK’s fiscal outlook. With the 10Y Gilt yield a few basis points lower in the aftermath, investors seem reasonably content with the Chancellor’s efforts to contain the government’s debt.

Part of the nudge lower in Gilt yields may also be due to investors’ positioning going into today, given there was a non-negligible risk that the Budget would fail to show credibility. For now there is some relief, but as mentioned above, we may very well face more uncertainty again in the near future.

At time of writing, Gilt yields are currently off around 4-5bp across the curve from their pre-OBR leak levels and EUR/GBP is off around 0.4%. At above 1.32, GBP/USD is back to levels seen in late October.

Over recent weeks Gilts and sterling have been pushed around by both fiscal credibility issues and by what today’s Budget could mean for BoE policy. While Gilt investors could have shown concern over the back-loaded nature of fiscal consolidation, instead they seemed to have backed Chancellor Reeves and the OBR’s path for narrower budget deficits over the next five years.

We had estimated that there was around a 10bp risk premium priced into Gilts ahead of this Budget, suggesting there could be a little more upside for Gilts from current levels. Helping the story as well has been the DMO’s Gilt issuance plans of £304bn and £275bn for '25/26 and '26/27 respectively, broadly as expected.

As for sterling, there had not been too much risk priced into the pound, but today’s Budget is not particularly contractionary in the short term and has not caused a significant repricing of the BoE terminal rate. This has provided a little support. Remember that it was the prospect of big income tax hikes and what it could mean for the BoE policy in 2026 which had hit sterling a couple of weeks ago.

We doubt sterling needs to rally too much further from here, however. We still look for the BoE to cut rates in December and expect the policy rate to be cut a little more quickly to the 3.25% area than the market currently prices. We expect EUR/GBP to find good support ahead of 0.87 and still favour a move towards 0.90 next year.

Perhaps one of the biggest turnarounds today has been the FTSE 100. This is up close to 1% from intraday lows led by financials. The two potential positives here are the Chancellor going ahead with plans to encourage ISAs to be used for investment purposes from 2027 onwards and the rumoured Stamp Duty Reserve Tax exemptions for UK listings over the next three years. These moves will be welcomed by the City, but a more durable rally in UK equities will surely hang on UK growth prospects – which still look unspectacular.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
